title: Chapter One: The Stranger
content:
## JEONG DAE-MYEONG

The child would not stop screaming.

Jeong Dae-myeong stood outside the birthing chamber and listened to it. Behind him the courtyard was dark, the wind cold, rattling the bare persimmon branches against the roof tiles.

*She is dead, then.*

He had known it before the midwife came out, her hands red to the wrists. A settling in the gut. Not grief. Resignation.

"The child lives, Master Jeong," the midwife said.

"Aye," he said. He did not ask about the mother.

He went inside.

The third wife lay on the mat with her eyes half-closed. Still. The blood beneath her had soaked through the bedding and into the wood.

The midwife placed the child in his arms.

Small. Red-faced and furious. Fists clenched. A boy, with a tuft of black hair plastered to a skull that seemed too large for his body. The crown of the head pulsed, and beneath it Dae-myeong could feel a warmth that had nothing to do with fever.

He held the child at arm's length.

*Born after I disgraced his mother. Born on the night she died.*

He handed the boy back to the midwife. Turned. Walked out.

The courtyard was no longer empty.

A man stood at the gate. Tall, thin, wrapped in a grey travelling cloak. Ears that rose to leaf-shaped points above his temples. A face all clean lines, no waste.

An Elf.

In Xinye County, in the backwater heart of Henan province, this was as likely as snow in summer. Dae-myeong had seen Elves once, at a tournament in Luoyang when he was a boy. Remote. Untouchable.

This one was standing at his gate, alone, in the middle of the night.

"I am a physician," the Elf said. His accent was strange, the vowels drawn long. "I heard a child born. I would examine it, if you permit."

Dae-myeong's hand went to his sword hilt. The Elf's eyes tracked the movement and dismissed it.

"You may enter," Dae-myeong said.

He led the stranger to the birthing room. The midwife pressed herself against the wall. The Elf ignored her. He took the child and held him in both hands, turning the boy slowly. His fingers traced the crown of the child's head, hovering just above the skin. A crease formed between his brows.

"The Baihui acupoint," the Elf said. "It is open."

"Speak plainly."

"The crown of his head. The gate through which energy passes between the body and the heavens." The Elf placed the child back down. "In most humans it is a pinprick. In this child it is a chasm. Wide open, like a door torn from its hinges."

Dae-myeong stared at him.

"He will not live past twenty," the Elf said. "Perhaps less. The energy of the world will pour through him unchecked, year after year, until his mind cannot bear the weight of it. He will lose himself. His thoughts will scatter. And then he will die."

Silence.

The child had stopped screaming. He lay on the mat, fists still clenched, his dark eyes open and fixed on the ceiling beam above him with an intensity that was unsettling in a newborn.

"Is this divine punishment?" Dae-myeong heard himself say. "His mother — I took her as a concubine against her family's wishes. She was from the Desolate Sect bloodline. I forced the match. Is this —"

"Divine punishment." The Elf's lip curled. "You ask a physician about the wrath of heaven. Even for an ignorant country squire, that is remarkably stupid."

Dae-myeong's face went dark.

"You are in my house."

"And your child is dying by degrees on that mat. Do you want to discuss etiquette, or do you want to listen?"

Dae-myeong said nothing. His jaw was clamped so tight the muscles in his neck stood out like cords.

The Elf continued as if the silence were agreement.

"The condition is similar to what your martial traditions call the Nine Yin Severed Pulse, but inverted. His meridians will not harden and close. They will remain open — too open. The natural energy of the world will flood through the upper dantian in his skull, and over time the accumulation will overwhelm his consciousness. Your people have a word for this, I believe."

"Ascension," Dae-myeong said quietly.

"Aye. Ascension. The Taoist monks speak of it as a blessing — the spirit departing for the celestial realm." The Elf's voice was flat and hard. "It is not a blessing. It is a drowning. The mind submerged beneath a tide of energy it cannot process. He will become an imbecile before he becomes a corpse."

A dog barked in the compound. Fell silent.

"Can it be treated?"

"Perhaps. If he were raised in Shaolin, the seventy-two arts might give him the discipline to regulate the flow. If an unorthodox sect took him, they would turn the open gate into a weapon — he would become a demon of considerable power before the condition consumed him." The Elf's fingers traced the edge of the swaddling cloth. "If he were brought to the Desolate Sect, and if the Sect Leader were inclined to generosity, the fruit of the World Tree might seal the gate entirely. But that is a sacred thing, not dispensed to the grandchildren of retired elders and country concubines."

"Then what do you suggest?"

The Elf looked at him. Contempt, and something that might have been pity.

"Love him," the Elf said. "Train him. Give him every advantage your provincial little family can muster. Teach him to fight, teach him to breathe, teach him to still his mind. And pray that he is clever enough to find a way to save himself, because you certainly cannot do it for him."

Dae-myeong's hand was on his sword hilt again.

"Get out."

The Elf turned and walked to the gate. At the threshold he paused, one hand on the frame.

"If raised in a place like this," he said, "with a father like you — there is no way but to die young in this countryside."

He stepped through the gate.

"Unless he awakens on his own."

The gate closed. The child on the mat opened and closed his small fists in the dark.
